summary: Chiwapa Bottom was a low area where my grandfather settled after coming down from Indiana. He bought land cheaply there, cut the big timber, and shipped it back to Indiana for Taylor Pearson's Lumber Company. My grandfather ended up owning a lot of land in that area and stayed in Mississippi because of it.

Indiana is where my grandfather originally came from before moving to Mississippi. He shipped the timber he cut from Chiwapa Bottom back to Indiana to Taylor Pearson's Lumber Company, where his brother-in-law received it. Indiana was the family’s original home, and it was connected to our lumber business.

Taylor Pearson's Lumber Company was the business in Indiana that received the timber my grandfather cut from Chiwapa Bottom. My grandfather would trim the limbs and load the timber onto flatcars to ship it there. His brother-in-law was involved with the company, which tied our family to the lumber industry.

I was born near Tupelo, Mississippi, on my grandfather’s place. It’s close to Shannon, Mississippi, where my family lived. Tupelo is part of the area where my early life and family roots were established.
Shannon, Mississippi, is near where I was born on my grandfather’s land close to Tupelo. It’s part of the region where my family lived and farmed. Shannon was a familiar place in my early years.

Emerson Buskirk was my grandfather who came down from Indiana and bought land in Chiwapa Bottom. He was the one who cut timber and shipped it back to Indiana. He was a key figure in our family’s history and land ownership in Mississippi.

Lydia Leatherman was my grandmother, married to Emerson Buskirk. She was part of the family that lived on the land near Tupelo and Shannon, Mississippi. Her family background influenced our upbringing.

My mother was Hermian Buskirk, born Hermian Pope, granddaughter of a country doctor. She grew up with stories of her mother driving her father during emergency calls at night. Hermian was a quiet and humble woman who balanced my father’s outspoken nature.

Dr. Crump was the country doctor in Smithville, Mississippi, connected to my mother’s family. My grandmother was a Crump, and the doctor’s work was part of our family heritage. The stories about night calls with lanterns were memorable from my mother’s side.

Smithville, Mississippi, was where Dr. Crump practiced medicine and where part of my mother’s family came from. It’s a place tied to my maternal heritage and the stories of my grandmother helping with emergency calls. Smithville is part of our family’s background.

My father, Bob Buskirk, was deeply involved in the United Methodist Church before he was called to preach. He served as Superintendent of Sunday school, taught classes, was on the board, and chaired the board at one time. The church was central to our family’s life and his ministry.

Jesus was central to my father’s calling and ministry. My dad believed strongly in the Scripture about Jesus giving apostles and pastors to equip the saints. His call to preach was deeply connected to his faith in Jesus and his desire to be a voice for Him.

Grover Meadows was a man my father played baseball with who later became a minister. When my dad stopped going to church for three years, Grover came to our home and invited us to a revival at Pleasant Grove. His sermon and encouragement led my father to accept his call to preach.

Pleasant Grove was the church where Grover Meadows started a revival that helped bring my father back to church. It was there that my dad accepted his call to preach, a very moving and emotional moment for our family. Pleasant Grove played a key role in my father’s ministry journey.

Bob Buskirk was my father, born in 1903, the middle of five sons. He was called to preach at eighteen while standing over his mother’s grave but delayed accepting it. Eventually, after a period of struggle and encouragement from Grover Meadows, he embraced his ministry and served in several Mississippi communities.

Randolph, Mississippi, was where my family moved when my father began preaching to fill in for a minister who was injured. I was in first grade then. The people there supported my father strongly and wanted him to stay, so we remained for two years.

Algoma, Mississippi, was where we moved after Randolph when I was in third grade. I remember being very aware of the poverty there. It was part of my early childhood and shaped my understanding of life and community.

I was named James Bradford Buskirk after my mother’s father and grew up being called Brad. It wasn’t until college, influenced by an English teacher, that I started going by James and then Jimmy. My name reflects my family heritage and my personal journey.

Nancy Merritt is my wife, originally from Calhoun City, Mississippi. We have lived in Oklahoma for forty years, and she has kept her southern accent despite trying to lose it. Nancy has been a strong partner in my life and ministry.

Calhoun City, Mississippi, is where my wife Nancy Merritt is from. It’s part of her southern roots that she has maintained even after moving to Oklahoma. Calhoun City represents her background and our shared southern heritage.

I joined the Scouts when I was thirteen after an incident where the assistant Scoutmaster caught me playing a prank. Becoming a Scout helped me overcome my timidity and develop public speaking and leadership skills. I eventually became senior patrol leader and learned many lessons that prepared me for ministry.

The assistant Scoutmaster who encouraged me to join the Scouts had just come out of the Air Force. His discipline and leadership helped guide me into Scouting and eventually into roles where I could develop confidence and speaking skills. The Air Force connection was indirect but influential.

December 7, 1941, was not directly mentioned in my story, but it was a significant historical event during my early life. My focus was more on family, ministry, and community during those years. The war era shaped many aspects of life around us.

Palestine, Mississippi, was not specifically mentioned in my story. However, it is part of the region where my family and ministry were rooted. Many small towns like Palestine shaped the rural Mississippi experience I grew up with.
